topic: Functioning of Parliament and State Legislature
subtopic: Assent to State Bills and Governor's Powers
title: Assent to State Bills and Governor's Powers
focus: Focuses on Articles 200 and 201 regarding the Governor's and President's roles in state bills, including discretionary powers and judicial review.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 007]
Note: Constitution of India (under Article 163 (2)) explicitly mentions that in certain spheres the Governor of a State acts in his/her own discretion. However, Constitution does not empower President to independently (suo motu) take up a State bill unless it has first been reserved and forwarded by the Governor.
### Constitutional Provisions Regarding Assent to State Bills
#### Governor's Responsibility for State's Bill (Article 200 of the Constitution of India)
1. State Legislature Passes A Bill.
2. Governor takes one of four actions:
* Grant Assent: Bill becomes law.
* Withhold Assent: Bill is rejected.
* Return for Reconsideration (Not for money bills) with suggestions for amendment: If the Legislature passes the bill again (with or without changes), the Governor MUST give assent.
* Reserve for President's Consideration.
*Note: Governor may do this as soon as possible.
#### President's Responsibility for Bills Reserved by the Governor (Article 201 of the Constitution of India)
1. Governor reserves Bill for President.
2. President takes one of three actions:
* Grant Assent: Bill becomes law.
* Withhold Assent: Bill is rejected.
* Return for Reconsideration (Not for money bills) with suggestions for amendment:
* Legislature must reconsider and pass the bill within 6 months.
* President makes final decision to give assent to reconsidered Bill (Grant Assent OR Withhold Assent).
Key Difference: Unlike Governor, President is NOT obligated to give assent to reconsidered Bills.
### Other Key Highlights of State of Tamil Nadu vs Governor of Tamil Nadu Judgement
| Aspect | Detail |
| :--- | :--- |
| Inaction is unconstitutional | Governor/President cannot exercise Pocket or Absolute Veto on Bills under Articles 200 & 201; inaction can be challenged via writ of mandamus. |
Note: Writ of mandamus cannot be issued:
* against private individual or body.
* to enforce departmental instruction that does not possess statutory force.
* when duty is discretionary to enforce contractual obligation.
* against President of India or State Governors.

[Page 008]
• - against Chief Justice of a High Court acting in a judicial capacity.
### Governor's Powers and Discretion regarding Bills
| Provision | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Governor can’t reserve re-enacted Bill | Governor may reserve a Bill only once; after re-enactment, it cannot be reserved again unless materially altered. |
| No absolute discretion under Article 200 | Governor is bound by Council of Minis...
